attributes.stem:
A 17-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with concerns about dark patches on her face that developed after severe acne six months ago. Her acne has now resolved with isotretinoin therapy which was completed two months ago, but the dark patches persist. She has tried whitening cream without effect. She has no other medical conditions and takes no regular medications. She works as a lifeguard at an outdoor pool.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are multiple well-demarcated, brown macules on both cheeks where her previous acne lesions were located. There are no active inflammatory lesions present.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation (PIH) secondary to resolved acne. PIH occurs when inflammation triggers increased melanin production, resulting in darkened patches in areas of previous inflammation. The condition is more common and more severe in individuals with darker skin types and can be exacerbated by sun exposure. Unfortunately whilst a common condition after trauma to the skin (especially with acne), it is difficult to achieve complete resolution with current treatments, and severe injury will lead to permanent hypopigmentation.

Topical hydroquinone 4% is the most appropriate first-line treatment for PIH. It works by inhibiting tyrosinase, thereby reducing melanin production. When combined with strict sun protection, this approach is both safe and effective. Treatment typically requires several months for optimal results, and patients should be advised about the importance of sun protection during treatment. Topical tretinoin and azelaic acid are also first-line alternatives.

Chemical peel would be too aggressive as a first-line treatment and could potentially worsen inflammation and hyperpigmentation if initiated too soon after completing isotretinoin therapy. It typically consists of 14% salicylic acid, 14% lactic acid and 14% resorcinol in alcohol solution, or may be solely tretinoin or salicylic acid peels. Chemical peels are typically considered as second-line therapy for epidermal pigmentation after failing topical treatments, has no effect in dermal pigmentation and has many side effects.

Oral tranexamic acid is typically reserved for more severe or resistant cases of melasma rather than post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation. It functions by reducing tyrosinase enzyme activity which reduces arachidonic acid and prostaglandins thus the tyrosinase. It carries risks of thromboembolism and requires careful patient selection.

Laser therapy should be considered only after failing conservative measures rather than first-line therapy. The safety data is still being explored though some cases demonstrate good response to laser therapy such as intense pulsed light.

Topical low-dose corticosteroid is not indicated for PIH as it does not address the underlying mechanism of increased melanin production. While corticosteroids can help with active inflammation, they may potentially thin the skin with prolonged use and are not effective for treating established hyperpigmentation.

The key principles in managing PIH include treating any underlying condition (already achieved in this case with isotretinoin), preventing further inflammation, and protecting from sun exposure. Given the patient's outdoor occupation, emphasis should be placed on strict sun protection measures including broad-spectrum sunscreen, protective clothing, and sun avoidance when possible.
learningPoints: Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation requires treatment of the underlying inflammatory condition before addressing the pigmentation.
Topical hydroquinone is first-line therapy for post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation in combination with strict sun protection.
Sun protection is essential to prevent worsening of post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation and improve treatment outcomes.
references: Nautiyal A, Wairkar S. Management of hyperpigmentation: Current treatments and emerging therapies.  Pigment Cell and Melanoma Research.  2001: 34 (6).  Available at: https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/pcmr.12986
Ngan V, Oakley A. Postinflammatory hyperpigmentation. DermNet NZ. 2015.  Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/postinflammatory-hyperpigmentation
